texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deployment of Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) technology is one of the implemented methods to reduce the emissions of greenhouse gases. The United Arab Emirates (UAE) is one of the countries that are striving towards vast economic growth, rapid urbanization and population boom that eventually resulted in an enormous increase in primary energy consumption and carbon emissions per capita. This study evaluates the effect of the CCS technology in large scale deployment on the natural gas (NG) market in the UAE. In the situation where the captured CO2 is used for enhancing the oil recovery (EOR), a benefit to the NG market will be added due to freeing up NG used in oil wellbores. The study reveals that for a 60% and 90% capture, a net amount of 5.32 and 21.16 million metric tonnes (mmtones) of NG will be saved yearly. Such saving will reduce the heavy dependence on importing NG and increase the opportunity of using NG for other industrial and domestic purposes.
texts.text: Deployment of Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) technology is one of the implemented methods to reduce the emissions of greenhouse gases. The United Arab Emirates (UAE) is one of the countries that are striving towards vast economic growth, rapid urbanization and population boom that eventually resulted in an enormous increase in primary energy consumption and carbon emissions per capita. This study evaluates the effect of the CCS technology in large scale deployment on the natural gas (NG) market in the UAE. In the situation where the captured CO2 is used for enhancing the oil recovery (EOR), a benefit to the NG market will be added due to freeing up NG used in oil wellbores. The study reveals that for a 60% and 90% capture, a net amount of 5.32 and 21.16 million metric tonnes (mmtones) of NG will be saved yearly. Such saving will reduce the heavy dependence on importing NG and increase the opportunity of using NG for other industrial and domestic purposes.

texts.orig: global sea level in the last century, the rapid decline in the level and thickness of Arctic sea ice over the last several decades, the diminish of glaciers around the world such as in Alps, Himalayas, Alaska and Africa, the increase in the severity  of  hurricanes,  droughts,  heat  waves  and  evaporation  of  water  supplies  are  some  evidence  proving  how climate  change  is  compelling  [1]. texts.text: global sea level in the last century, the rapid decline in the level and thickness of Arctic sea ice over the last several decades, the diminish of glaciers around the world such as in Alps, Himalayas, Alaska and Africa, the increase in the severity  of  hurricanes,  droughts,  heat  waves  and  evaporation  of  water  supplies  are  some  evidence  proving  how climate  change  is  compelling  [1].  Global  warming  is  a  crisis  which  damages  biodiversity  and  ecosystems  and eventually the human food system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon  dioxide  (CO2)  is  a  major  greenhouse  gas  with  a  high  anthropogenic  emission  and  plays  the  major contribution  factor  to  global  warming,  caused  mainly  by  fossil  fuel  combustion.  The  Intergovernmental  Panel  on Climate Change (IPCC) states that global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions must be reduced by 50 to 80 percent by 2050 to avoid devastating consequences of global warming [2]. There are several methods to reduce CO2 emissions such as increasing energy efficiency, implementing CO2 Capture and Storage (CCS) technology, the usage of bio fuels  as  an  alternative  to  fossil  fuels,  and  producing  electricity  from  nuclear  power  and  renewables.  However; because the world economy depends on fossil fuels to meet its primary energy requirements and because of the rise in population and economic growth which eventually result in increasing the electricity demand, reductions of CO2 emissions generated from the power sector will add an effective contribution to the decrease of global emissions.
texts.text: Carbon  dioxide  (CO2)  is  a  major  greenhouse  gas  with  a  high  anthropogenic  emission  and  plays  the  major contribution  factor  to  global  warming,  caused  mainly  by  fossil  fuel  combustion.  The  Intergovernmental  Panel  on Climate Change (IPCC) states that global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions must be reduced by 50 to 80 percent by 2050 to avoid devastating consequences of global warming [2]. There are several methods to reduce CO2 emissions such as increasing energy efficiency, implementing CO2 Capture and Storage (CCS) technology, the usage of bio fuels  as  an  alternative  to  fossil  fuels,  and  producing  electricity  from  nuclear  power  and  renewables.  However; because the world economy depends on fossil fuels to meet its primary energy requirements and because of the rise in population and economic growth which eventually result in increasing the electricity demand, reductions of CO2 emissions generated from the power sector will add an effective contribution to the decrease of global emissions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Applying the technology of CO2 capture, transport and storage (CCS) from fossil fuel power plants is one of the viable options to mitigate climate change consequences in the long term. It consists of three stages: capturing CO2 from the source station, transporting it through pipelines after compression, and storing it in geological reservoirs. Regarding the carbon capture technologies, the post combustion technology has a unique advantage because it is easy  to  be  retrofitted  with  existing  power  plants  compared  to  Pre-combustion;  and  the  Oxy-Fuel  Combustion Capture  technologies  [3].  In  addition,  post  combustion  has  a  higher  thermal  efficiency  and  lower  electricity  cost compared  to  Pre  and  Oxy-fuel  combustion  [3].  Also,  it  has  the  advantage  of  high  capture  efficiency,  scale  up feasibility and high selectivity [4]. This implies the feasibility of post combustion at a commercial scale.
texts.text: Applying the technology of CO2 capture, transport and storage (CCS) from fossil fuel power plants is one of the viable options to mitigate climate change consequences in the long term. It consists of three stages: capturing CO2 from the source station, transporting it through pipelines after compression, and storing it in geological reservoirs. Regarding the carbon capture technologies, the post combustion technology has a unique advantage because it is easy  to  be  retrofitted  with  existing  power  plants  compared  to  Pre-combustion;  and  the  Oxy-Fuel  Combustion Capture  technologies  [3].  In  addition,  post  combustion  has  a  higher  thermal  efficiency  and  lower  electricity  cost compared  to  Pre  and  Oxy-fuel  combustion  [3].  Also,  it  has  the  advantage  of  high  capture  efficiency,  scale  up feasibility and high selectivity [4]. This implies the feasibility of post combustion at a commercial scale.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  post  combustion  process  takes  place  after  the  combustion  of  fossil  fuel  where  CO 2 is  removed  from  the combustion products before being emitted to the atmosphere. Because of the low partial pressure of CO2 in the flue gas, separation by chemical absorption is considered to be the most efficient separation technology for CO2 postcombustion  capture  [5].  Monoethanolamine  (MEA)  verified  its  feasibility  beyond  other  solvents  used  in  the chemical absorption. The main advantage of MEA as an absorbent is that it shows a fast chemical reaction rate with CO2.  Also it has low viscosity and density, in addition to its availability among the industrial applications and being relatively cheap compared to other Amine based solvents [6]. On the other hand, MEA has some drawbacks since it has low carbon dioxide loading capacity (Kg CO2 absorbed per Kg solvent) and it requires high energy requirement for the regeneration in addition to its possibility to be degraded in the presence of SO2 and O2 in the flue gas causing high equipment corrosion rate [7].
texts.text: The  post  combustion  process  takes  place  after  the  combustion  of  fossil  fuel  where  CO 2 is  removed  from  the combustion products before being emitted to the atmosphere. Because of the low partial pressure of CO2 in the flue gas, separation by chemical absorption is considered to be the most efficient separation technology for CO2 postcombustion  capture  [5].  Monoethanolamine  (MEA)  verified  its  feasibility  beyond  other  solvents  used  in  the chemical absorption. The main advantage of MEA as an absorbent is that it shows a fast chemical reaction rate with CO2.  Also it has low viscosity and density, in addition to its availability among the industrial applications and being relatively cheap compared to other Amine based solvents [6]. On the other hand, MEA has some drawbacks since it has low carbon dioxide loading capacity (Kg CO2 absorbed per Kg solvent) and it requires high energy requirement for the regeneration in addition to its possibility to be degraded in the presence of SO2 and O2 in the flue gas causing high equipment corrosion rate [7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The United Arab Emirates (UAE) is one of the countries that are striving towards vast economic growth, rapid urbanization and population boom that eventually resulted in an enormous increase in primary energy consumption and carbon emissions per capita [8]. Adding to this, the increase in total water consumption is a growing problem in UAE with a heavy reliance on thermal desalination plants for water supply. There are 70 desalination plants in the UAE that generate about 14 % of the total global capacity and mainly these plants receive their energy supply from the  power  plant  [9].  The  country  has  since  diversified  its  economic  sectors  to  reduce  the  dependency  on hydrocarbons as the sole economic driver, making its economy the most diversified in the region. In order to meet up the growth in the energy demand, the UAE government has established a renewable energy sector and made a policy to meet 7% of its energy from renewables while 23-25% from nuclear energy by 2020 [8]. For the Carbon Capture and Storage technology (CCS), in the last couple of years, the UAE has decided implementing this technology to capture CO2 from large source points such as power, aluminum and steel production plants. A serious step towards such implementation is the joint venture project between Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC) and Masdar Company which led to the creation of a new company, Abu Dhabi Carbon Capture Company. The first project is Al Reyada and is currently under the commissioning phase and will be  fully operated by the first quarter of 2017 to capture  annually  around  0.8  million  metric  tons  of  CO 2 from  the  Emirates  Steel  plant  located  in  Mussafah.  The
texts.text: The United Arab Emirates (UAE) is one of the countries that are striving towards vast economic growth, rapid urbanization and population boom that eventually resulted in an enormous increase in primary energy consumption and carbon emissions per capita [8]. Adding to this, the increase in total water consumption is a growing problem in UAE with a heavy reliance on thermal desalination plants for water supply. There are 70 desalination plants in the UAE that generate about 14 % of the total global capacity and mainly these plants receive their energy supply from the  power  plant  [9].  The  country  has  since  diversified  its  economic  sectors  to  reduce  the  dependency  on hydrocarbons as the sole economic driver, making its economy the most diversified in the region. In order to meet up the growth in the energy demand, the UAE government has established a renewable energy sector and made a policy to meet 7% of its energy from renewables while 23-25% from nuclear energy by 2020 [8]. For the Carbon Capture and Storage technology (CCS), in the last couple of years, the UAE has decided implementing this technology to capture CO2 from large source points such as power, aluminum and steel production plants. A serious step towards such implementation is the joint venture project between Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC) and Masdar Company which led to the creation of a new company, Abu Dhabi Carbon Capture Company. The first project is Al Reyada and is currently under the commissioning phase and will be  fully operated by the first quarter of 2017 to capture  annually  around  0.8  million  metric  tons  of  CO 2 from  the  Emirates  Steel  plant  located  in  Mussafah.  The

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Iman Ustadi et al. /   Energy Procedia   114 (  2017  )   6366 - 6376
texts.text: Iman Ustadi et al. /   Energy Procedia   114 (  2017  )   6366 - 6376

texts.label: text
texts.orig: captured  CO2  stream  will  be  further  compressed,  dehydrated  and  then  pumped  through  50  km  of  pipeline  to  be injected  in  an  onshore  oil  field,  operated  by  Abu  Dhabi  Company  for  onshore  oil  operations  (ADCO)  [10].  The captured CO2 will be utilized to enhance the production of oil and thus replacing the current water and natural gas injection. In this case, it is approximated that the use of CO2 for enhanced oil recovery (EOR) process could help in saving  up  to  40%  of  the  total  natural  gas  that  could  be  used  for  power  generation  in  the  UAE  or  for  income generation by exporting the gas to needed countries [11].
texts.text: captured  CO2  stream  will  be  further  compressed,  dehydrated  and  then  pumped  through  50  km  of  pipeline  to  be injected  in  an  onshore  oil  field,  operated  by  Abu  Dhabi  Company  for  onshore  oil  operations  (ADCO)  [10].  The captured CO2 will be utilized to enhance the production of oil and thus replacing the current water and natural gas injection. In this case, it is approximated that the use of CO2 for enhanced oil recovery (EOR) process could help in saving  up  to  40%  of  the  total  natural  gas  that  could  be  used  for  power  generation  in  the  UAE  or  for  income generation by exporting the gas to needed countries [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Assessing the future role of CCS as one of the abatement policies should take into consideration different factors; such as technical uncertainties, economic uncertainties, and the future market's situation. The latter includes looking at the trend of natural gas (NG) in terms of supply and demand, imports and exports, and the market of NG prices. The aim of this work is to analyze the potential for CCS large scale deployment in the UAE. In addition, it will evaluate the effect of the CCS technology in large scale deployment on the NG market in the UAE. The evaluation is achieved by looking at the forecasted power generation/demands as they are directly affected with the supply of the NG,  in  addition  to  the  NG  market's  situation  in  the  UAE.  In  addition,  the  analysis  will  consider  different  CO 2 capture and CCS deployment long term scenarios in the UAE.
texts.text: Assessing the future role of CCS as one of the abatement policies should take into consideration different factors; such as technical uncertainties, economic uncertainties, and the future market's situation. The latter includes looking at the trend of natural gas (NG) in terms of supply and demand, imports and exports, and the market of NG prices. The aim of this work is to analyze the potential for CCS large scale deployment in the UAE. In addition, it will evaluate the effect of the CCS technology in large scale deployment on the NG market in the UAE. The evaluation is achieved by looking at the forecasted power generation/demands as they are directly affected with the supply of the NG,  in  addition  to  the  NG  market's  situation  in  the  UAE.  In  addition,  the  analysis  will  consider  different  CO 2 capture and CCS deployment long term scenarios in the UAE.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1. Electricity generation in the UAE
texts.text: 1.1. Electricity generation in the UAE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the UAE, electricity is produced from various fuel types such as natural gas, crude oil, and gas oil. In addition, renewable  energy  technologies  were  adopted  in  the  past  years  such  as  solar  and  wind  besides  nuclear  energy. Nowadays, the dominant source of fuel used for electricity generation in UAE is natural gas (96 %) followed by Gas oil (3%), and only 1% for fuel oil [12]. Generally, there has been an increase in gas usage in the UAE's for power generation. This is illustrated in Figure 1. It reveals that the UAE will require 93% of the total natural gas production in generating electricity by 2020. Further projections indicate that the total amount of natural gas produced in the UAE will be insufficient in the production of electricity should the current trend continue [13].
texts.text: In the UAE, electricity is produced from various fuel types such as natural gas, crude oil, and gas oil. In addition, renewable  energy  technologies  were  adopted  in  the  past  years  such  as  solar  and  wind  besides  nuclear  energy. Nowadays, the dominant source of fuel used for electricity generation in UAE is natural gas (96 %) followed by Gas oil (3%), and only 1% for fuel oil [12]. Generally, there has been an increase in gas usage in the UAE's for power generation. This is illustrated in Figure 1. It reveals that the UAE will require 93% of the total natural gas production in generating electricity by 2020. Further projections indicate that the total amount of natural gas produced in the UAE will be insufficient in the production of electricity should the current trend continue [13].

texts.orig: Figure 1: Amount of gas consumed in generating UAE's electricity against the total production  (1990-2020) [13] .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2. Natural gas market in the UAE
texts.text: 1.2. Natural gas market in the UAE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: UAE is the seventh largest country globally in terms of both oil and natural gas reserves (approximated at 215 trillion cubic feet (Tcf) for the NG) and is a member of the Gas Exporting Countries Forum (GECF). However; in 2008, the UAE became a net importer of NG as its consumption increased and projected to play a key role in the development of UAE in the years to come [8].
texts.text: UAE is the seventh largest country globally in terms of both oil and natural gas reserves (approximated at 215 trillion cubic feet (Tcf) for the NG) and is a member of the Gas Exporting Countries Forum (GECF). However; in 2008, the UAE became a net importer of NG as its consumption increased and projected to play a key role in the development of UAE in the years to come [8].

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Iman Ustadi et al. /   Energy Procedia   114 (  2017  )   6366 - 6376
texts.text: Iman Ustadi et al. /   Energy Procedia   114 (  2017  )   6366 - 6376

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 2 depicts the UAE's demand and production of natural gas. As can be noted, from the year 2007 onwards, the demand for natural gas outstripped the production where the consumption of NG increased yearly by an average of 6% between 2004 and 2013. The consumption obtained a record of more than 2.4 Tcf in 2013 whereas the  dry  natural  gas  production  rose  to  1.9  [14].  This  disparity  is  a  result  of  several  reasons.  First  is  the  large dependency on natural  gas  for  power  generation  in  the  UAE,  which  is  growing  in  parallel  to  the  economic  and demographic growth. Although the UAE is making an enormous success in diversifying its primary energy sources, the  demand  is  likely  to  depend  heavily  on  the  UAE's  natural  gas  resources.  Another  reason  is  the  considerable domestic usage of NG for oil field re-injection to maintain reservoir pressure and oil production capacity. In 2012, the UAE re-injected an approximate of 30% of gross NG production for enhancing oil recovery (EOR) operations [15].  Therefore,  the  application  of  CCS  technology  in  the  UAE  will  definitely  help  in  reducing  the  enormous consumption on NG for oil field re-injection.
texts.text: Figure 2 depicts the UAE's demand and production of natural gas. As can be noted, from the year 2007 onwards, the demand for natural gas outstripped the production where the consumption of NG increased yearly by an average of 6% between 2004 and 2013. The consumption obtained a record of more than 2.4 Tcf in 2013 whereas the  dry  natural  gas  production  rose  to  1.9  [14].  This  disparity  is  a  result  of  several  reasons.  First  is  the  large dependency on natural  gas  for  power  generation  in  the  UAE,  which  is  growing  in  parallel  to  the  economic  and demographic growth. Although the UAE is making an enormous success in diversifying its primary energy sources, the  demand  is  likely  to  depend  heavily  on  the  UAE's  natural  gas  resources.  Another  reason  is  the  considerable domestic usage of NG for oil field re-injection to maintain reservoir pressure and oil production capacity. In 2012, the UAE re-injected an approximate of 30% of gross NG production for enhancing oil recovery (EOR) operations [15].  Therefore,  the  application  of  CCS  technology  in  the  UAE  will  definitely  help  in  reducing  the  enormous consumption on NG for oil field re-injection.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 2: UAE Natural Gas production and consumption (2004-2013)[14]
texts.text: Figure 2: UAE Natural Gas production and consumption (2004-2013)[14]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to meet the growth in the demand for NG, in 1999 the government of Abu Dhabi decided to import gas from Qatar; therefore, the Dolphin Gas Project was implemented. The Project has started in 2007 and includes production and processing of natural gas from Qatar's offshore North Field then transporting the processed gas by building an 800 km undersea pipeline from the North field to Abu Dhabi's Taweelah power station. From Abu Dhabi, the gas would be piped overland to Jebel Ali and to Oman [16].
texts.text: In order to meet the growth in the demand for NG, in 1999 the government of Abu Dhabi decided to import gas from Qatar; therefore, the Dolphin Gas Project was implemented. The Project has started in 2007 and includes production and processing of natural gas from Qatar's offshore North Field then transporting the processed gas by building an 800 km undersea pipeline from the North field to Abu Dhabi's Taweelah power station. From Abu Dhabi, the gas would be piped overland to Jebel Ali and to Oman [16].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 3 shows the trend of NG imports and exports in the UAE from 2004 to 2013. As can be noted, in 2013 there was a net deficit of around 400 billion cubic feet in NG in the UAE [14]. A major reason behind that is due to the sourness (sulfur-rich) of the available natural gas in UAE, which makes it corrosive; thus, difficult and expensive to be  processed.  On  the  other  hand;  the  exports  of  NG  remained  fairly  constant  due  to  the  'long-term  supply agreements' between the UAE and other countries such as India, Japan, Taiwan and Kuwait [17]. These agreements call for the export of liquefied natural gas despite the domestic shortage of the product. For example, in 2013, the UAE exported an amount of 284 Bcf of NG; 90% was mostly in the form of LNG, to Japan [14].
texts.text: Figure 3 shows the trend of NG imports and exports in the UAE from 2004 to 2013. As can be noted, in 2013 there was a net deficit of around 400 billion cubic feet in NG in the UAE [14]. A major reason behind that is due to the sourness (sulfur-rich) of the available natural gas in UAE, which makes it corrosive; thus, difficult and expensive to be  processed.  On  the  other  hand;  the  exports  of  NG  remained  fairly  constant  due  to  the  'long-term  supply agreements' between the UAE and other countries such as India, Japan, Taiwan and Kuwait [17]. These agreements call for the export of liquefied natural gas despite the domestic shortage of the product. For example, in 2013, the UAE exported an amount of 284 Bcf of NG; 90% was mostly in the form of LNG, to Japan [14].

texts.orig: Figure 3: Trend of UAE NG imports and exports (2004-2014) [15]
texts.text: Figure 3: Trend of UAE NG imports and exports (2004-2014) [15]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Such deficit is expected to continue for the years between 2015 and 2020 as the gas demand in UAE will exceed the current supply, despite the importation of Qatari gas via the Dolphin pipeline. It is projected that the total consumption of natural gas will reach 107.5 Bcm/yr by 2020 in comparison to the production which is projected to be 78.1 Bcm/yr [18]. This is to conclude that for the current time, the UAE faces an impending domestic gas crisis that  threatens  the  reliability  of  its  electricity  supply.  However;  despite  the  burden  of  such  deficit,  the  UAE's government is seeking to develop refinement facilities in order to increase domestic production of NG reserves that could help in meeting the massive growing demand and thus decrease the dependency on NG imports. For instance; ADNOC has established  a  number  of  several  ongoing  projects  such  as  the  Onshore  Gas  Development  (OGD), Integrated Gas Development (IGD), and Offshore Associated Gas (OAG) in order to upsurge the production of NG reserves. Moreover; ADNOC is achieving a large-scale sour-gas development at the Shah gas field. This production project  came  on  table  in  January  2015  and  is  expected  to  produce  504  million  cubic  feet  per  day  (MMcf/d)  of marketable dry natural gas treated from the gross production of 1 billion cubic feet (Bcf) of ulta-sour gas and 50,000 bbl/d of liquid natural gas [15].
texts.text: Such deficit is expected to continue for the years between 2015 and 2020 as the gas demand in UAE will exceed the current supply, despite the importation of Qatari gas via the Dolphin pipeline. It is projected that the total consumption of natural gas will reach 107.5 Bcm/yr by 2020 in comparison to the production which is projected to be 78.1 Bcm/yr [18]. This is to conclude that for the current time, the UAE faces an impending domestic gas crisis that  threatens  the  reliability  of  its  electricity  supply.  However;  despite  the  burden  of  such  deficit,  the  UAE's government is seeking to develop refinement facilities in order to increase domestic production of NG reserves that could help in meeting the massive growing demand and thus decrease the dependency on NG imports. For instance; ADNOC has established  a  number  of  several  ongoing  projects  such  as  the  Onshore  Gas  Development  (OGD), Integrated Gas Development (IGD), and Offshore Associated Gas (OAG) in order to upsurge the production of NG reserves. Moreover; ADNOC is achieving a large-scale sour-gas development at the Shah gas field. This production project  came  on  table  in  January  2015  and  is  expected  to  produce  504  million  cubic  feet  per  day  (MMcf/d)  of marketable dry natural gas treated from the gross production of 1 billion cubic feet (Bcf) of ulta-sour gas and 50,000 bbl/d of liquid natural gas [15].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Analysis of the Natural Gas market and CCUS technology
texts.text: 2. Analysis of the Natural Gas market and CCUS technology

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After looking at the power generation path besides the natural gas demand and supply chain in the UAE, it is to conclude that the electricity market in the UAE is changing. This is a result of a rise in the electricity demand and the  growth in the power plant fleet  where a large share of the capacity  will be  met by alternative and clean sources  of  energy.  In  addition,  UAE  is  suffering  from  the  high  CO 2 emissions  per  capita.  One  of  the  effective technologies applied in the UAE to achieve the CO2 emissions reduction target and a long term sustainable energy supply is the technology of Carbon Capture Utilization and Sequestration (CCUS). In Abu Dhabi, there is currently one large scale CCUS project, which is in the commissioning phase. This project is designed to capture an amount of 41.5 MMSCFD of CO2 from the Emirates Steel plant and utilize it to be injected at the Rumaitha and Bab oil fields, operated by Abu Dhabi company for onshore operations (ADCO), for enhance the oil recovery. ADNOC is already studying the feasibility of other CCS projects and the priority is given for capturing the CO2 from its natural gas power plants. The introduction of CCS in the UAE's market will alter an anticipated change in the CO2 prices, the  investment  cost  of  the  power  plant,  the  natural  gas  demand  trend  and  prices  in  addition  to  the  possibility  of introducing CO2 taxes or the support of government incentives. This section of the paper will perform a high level analysis to investigate the possible impact of deploying the CCS technology on the NG market in the UAE. The analysis is performed by implementing various capture percentages. In every scenario, the amount on NG consumed in  the  power  and  the  capture  plant  will  be  analyzed  in  parallel  to  the  amount  of  CO 2 captured  in  every  capture scenario.  This  is  to  note  down  that  there  are  various  parameters  that  might  have  either  a  direct  or  indirect implications  on  the  NG  market.  However,  for  the  current  time  and  based  on  the  available  data,  only  the  capture percentage will be studied.
texts.text: After looking at the power generation path besides the natural gas demand and supply chain in the UAE, it is to conclude that the electricity market in the UAE is changing. This is a result of a rise in the electricity demand and the  growth in the power plant fleet  where a large share of the capacity  will be  met by alternative and clean sources  of  energy.  In  addition,  UAE  is  suffering  from  the  high  CO 2 emissions  per  capita.  One  of  the  effective technologies applied in the UAE to achieve the CO2 emissions reduction target and a long term sustainable energy supply is the technology of Carbon Capture Utilization and Sequestration (CCUS). In Abu Dhabi, there is currently one large scale CCUS project, which is in the commissioning phase. This project is designed to capture an amount of 41.5 MMSCFD of CO2 from the Emirates Steel plant and utilize it to be injected at the Rumaitha and Bab oil fields, operated by Abu Dhabi company for onshore operations (ADCO), for enhance the oil recovery. ADNOC is already studying the feasibility of other CCS projects and the priority is given for capturing the CO2 from its natural gas power plants. The introduction of CCS in the UAE's market will alter an anticipated change in the CO2 prices, the  investment  cost  of  the  power  plant,  the  natural  gas  demand  trend  and  prices  in  addition  to  the  possibility  of introducing CO2 taxes or the support of government incentives. This section of the paper will perform a high level analysis to investigate the possible impact of deploying the CCS technology on the NG market in the UAE. The analysis is performed by implementing various capture percentages. In every scenario, the amount on NG consumed in  the  power  and  the  capture  plant  will  be  analyzed  in  parallel  to  the  amount  of  CO 2 captured  in  every  capture scenario.  This  is  to  note  down  that  there  are  various  parameters  that  might  have  either  a  direct  or  indirect implications  on  the  NG  market.  However,  for  the  current  time  and  based  on  the  available  data,  only  the  capture percentage will be studied.

This  is  to  note  down  that  this  graph  did  not  consider  the  application  of  other  renewable technologies in the UAE's energy mix such as solar, wind in addition to the nuclear energy. However, in the coming sections, the contribution of the renewable sector is considered as it will affect the emissions rate of CO2 and the consumption of NG.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.2 Expected natural gas consumption without CCS
texts.text: 2.1.2 Expected natural gas consumption without CCS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Moving towards the fuel consumption, natural gas is massively used in the production of electricity in the UAE at a percentage of around 96% compared to the other fuels such as crude oil and gas oil [12]. In order to calculate the consumption of natural gas as a fuel combusted in the power plant, the value of the NG mass per MW of electricity generated is required. Using the values for the annual gas consumption and electricity demand in the UAE, it was found that on a yearly basis, the average amount of NG needed to generate 1 MW of electricity is averagely around 0.17  tonnes/hr.  This  value  is  maintaining  a  power  plant  efficiency  of  around  42%  which  is  reasonable  for  the ambient temperature condition in the UAE. Another reason for lower power plant efficiency in the UAE compared to the European countries is because of the integration between the desalination and the NGCC power plants where the former receives its power from the latter through a low pressure steam extracted from the steam turbine. Figure 5 presents the forecasted amount of NG consumed in the power plant to meet the forecasted demand of electricity
texts.text: Moving towards the fuel consumption, natural gas is massively used in the production of electricity in the UAE at a percentage of around 96% compared to the other fuels such as crude oil and gas oil [12]. In order to calculate the consumption of natural gas as a fuel combusted in the power plant, the value of the NG mass per MW of electricity generated is required. Using the values for the annual gas consumption and electricity demand in the UAE, it was found that on a yearly basis, the average amount of NG needed to generate 1 MW of electricity is averagely around 0.17  tonnes/hr.  This  value  is  maintaining  a  power  plant  efficiency  of  around  42%  which  is  reasonable  for  the ambient temperature condition in the UAE. Another reason for lower power plant efficiency in the UAE compared to the European countries is because of the integration between the desalination and the NGCC power plants where the former receives its power from the latter through a low pressure steam extracted from the steam turbine. Figure 5 presents the forecasted amount of NG consumed in the power plant to meet the forecasted demand of electricity

texts.orig: 2.1.3 Expected CO2 emissions
texts.text: 2.1.3 Expected CO2 emissions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  calculating  the  expected  emissions  of  CO 2 (generated  from  power  plants)  and  forecasted  until  2030,  the production from nuclear plant is considered as generator units of 1.4GWe each and assumed to be integrated to the transmission system through 2017-2020 to meet a total capacity of 5.6 GW [8]. Figure 6 shows the values obtained for the calculation of CO2 emissions generated from the natural gas fired plants in the UAE.

Figure 6 shows the values obtained for the calculation of CO2 emissions generated from the natural gas fired plants in the UAE.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 Expected natural gas consumption scenarios with CCS
texts.text: 2.2 Expected natural gas consumption scenarios with CCS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After defining the forecasted trend of energy demand, NG demand and CO2 emissions in the UAE without CCS, this section will analyze the effect of CCS technology on the NG market. To achieve this, three different scenarios are  introduced  in  this  analysis.  First  is  a  smooth  capture  percentage  from  the  power  plant  which  stands  at  30%, second is an intermediate capture percentage of 60% , and the third is a tough CO2 removal at 90%. The selection of these percentages didn't take into account the capture cost and the levelized cost of electricity; yet it was selected based on the aim of having a variation in the percentage level of CO2 removal. Moreover; in these 3 scenarios of capture, two situations are presented and studied. The first is when the captured CO2 is used for sequestration and other  industrial  uses  (where  NG  is  not  utilized)  while  the  second  is  when  using  the  CO 2 for  enhancing  the  oil recovery that results in saving the NG used for pressurization in the wellbores.
texts.text: After defining the forecasted trend of energy demand, NG demand and CO2 emissions in the UAE without CCS, this section will analyze the effect of CCS technology on the NG market. To achieve this, three different scenarios are  introduced  in  this  analysis.  First  is  a  smooth  capture  percentage  from  the  power  plant  which  stands  at  30%, second is an intermediate capture percentage of 60% , and the third is a tough CO2 removal at 90%. The selection of these percentages didn't take into account the capture cost and the levelized cost of electricity; yet it was selected based on the aim of having a variation in the percentage level of CO2 removal. Moreover; in these 3 scenarios of capture, two situations are presented and studied. The first is when the captured CO2 is used for sequestration and other  industrial  uses  (where  NG  is  not  utilized)  while  the  second  is  when  using  the  CO 2 for  enhancing  the  oil recovery that results in saving the NG used for pressurization in the wellbores.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.1 Captured CO2 for sequestration & industrial uses
texts.text: 2.2.1 Captured CO2 for sequestration & industrial uses

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In every scenario of capture, the CO2 emission rate was calculated and compared to the base case scenario where no removal is applied. Figure 7 presents the amount of CO2 removed in million metric tons for 30, 60 and 90% of capture.  As  can  be  noted,  the  rate  of  CO 2 emitted  to  the  atmosphere  will  be  degraded  if  a  capture  percentage  is applied  on  the  power  plant  and  will  follow  a  growing  relation  with  the  increase  in  the  capture  percentage.  For instance, in 2030, with the absence of capture technology in the base case; an amount of 66.6 million metric tons will  be  emitted  to  the  atmosphere,  while  if  the  capture  plant  is  implemented  on  power  stations,  then  the  CO 2 emission  rate  will  be  reduced  to  46.6,  26.6,  and  6.67  million  metric  tons  at  30%,  60%,  and  90%  capture, respectively.
texts.text: In every scenario of capture, the CO2 emission rate was calculated and compared to the base case scenario where no removal is applied. Figure 7 presents the amount of CO2 removed in million metric tons for 30, 60 and 90% of capture.  As  can  be  noted,  the  rate  of  CO 2 emitted  to  the  atmosphere  will  be  degraded  if  a  capture  percentage  is applied  on  the  power  plant  and  will  follow  a  growing  relation  with  the  increase  in  the  capture  percentage.  For instance, in 2030, with the absence of capture technology in the base case; an amount of 66.6 million metric tons will  be  emitted  to  the  atmosphere,  while  if  the  capture  plant  is  implemented  on  power  stations,  then  the  CO 2 emission  rate  will  be  reduced  to  46.6,  26.6,  and  6.67  million  metric  tons  at  30%,  60%,  and  90%  capture, respectively.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Iman Ustadi et al. /   Energy Procedia   114 (  2017  )   6366 - 6376
texts.text: Iman Ustadi et al. /   Energy Procedia   114 (  2017  )   6366 - 6376

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 7:CO2 Emissions Reduction using the capture technology in the UAE
texts.text: Figure 7:CO2 Emissions Reduction using the capture technology in the UAE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From  an  environmental  perspective,  it  is  of  a  natural  consequence  that  the  more  capture  percentage  is applied the less CO2 will be emitted to the atmosphere which is a better remedy for a cleaner environment. In this paper, the emphasis on the reduction of CO2 emissions rate was considered with the link to the natural gas demand and market. However, in the future work, before the selection of the capture percentage is made; some factors that have an effect on the power plants of capturing CO2 should also be considered. For instance, a sensitivity analysis for the reduction in power plant's efficiency, the amount of CO2 emitted from the plant, the increase in the capital cost and cost of electricity generation, and the cost of the fuel should all be taken into account.
texts.text: From  an  environmental  perspective,  it  is  of  a  natural  consequence  that  the  more  capture  percentage  is applied the less CO2 will be emitted to the atmosphere which is a better remedy for a cleaner environment. In this paper, the emphasis on the reduction of CO2 emissions rate was considered with the link to the natural gas demand and market. However, in the future work, before the selection of the capture percentage is made; some factors that have an effect on the power plants of capturing CO2 should also be considered. For instance, a sensitivity analysis for the reduction in power plant's efficiency, the amount of CO2 emitted from the plant, the increase in the capital cost and cost of electricity generation, and the cost of the fuel should all be taken into account.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.2 Energy required for CCS
texts.text: 2.2.2 Energy required for CCS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After  calculating  the  reduced  values  of  CO 2 emissions  resulted  from  capture  technology,  the  effect  of  such capture  percentage  on  the  consumed  amount  of  natural  gas  should  be  studied.  This  is  achieved  by  finding  the amount of additional energy required to operate the power plant with CCS capturing technologies. For example, in the NGCC power plant, the reboiler duty per kg of CO2 captured increases as the capture percentage increases. This is because as the capture percentage increases, the solvent flowrate required increases which results in increasing the sensible heat requirements in the system and the CO2 regeneration energy will be higher [20].
texts.text: After  calculating  the  reduced  values  of  CO 2 emissions  resulted  from  capture  technology,  the  effect  of  such capture  percentage  on  the  consumed  amount  of  natural  gas  should  be  studied.  This  is  achieved  by  finding  the amount of additional energy required to operate the power plant with CCS capturing technologies. For example, in the NGCC power plant, the reboiler duty per kg of CO2 captured increases as the capture percentage increases. This is because as the capture percentage increases, the solvent flowrate required increases which results in increasing the sensible heat requirements in the system and the CO2 regeneration energy will be higher [20].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on literature findings; in order to capture 90% of the CO2 emitted, additional 14.7% of electricity capacity is  required  [21].  For  the  other  capture  percentages  (30%  and  60%),  a  liner  relation  was  simply  assumed  which resulted  in  an  extra  requirement  of  4.9%  and  9.8%  of  electricity  requirement  for  the  CO 2 removal,  respectively. Translating the additional electricity needed to capture CO2 emissions into additional amount of NG needed as a fuel, the power plant efficiency of 42% is considered. Such efficiency includes the utilizing percentage of the burned NG for the combined cycle gas turbine. For instance, at 90% capture percentage, the 14.7% increase in electricity requires  an  increase  in  the  NG  feed  stream  by  a  percentage  of  14.7%  in  addition  to  an  extra  power  due  to  the efficiency loss of the burned NG. Figure 8 presents a comparison between the quantity of NG consumed in the base case to meet the electricity demand and the 3 scenarios of CO2 capture till the year 2030.
texts.text: Based on literature findings; in order to capture 90% of the CO2 emitted, additional 14.7% of electricity capacity is  required  [21].  For  the  other  capture  percentages  (30%  and  60%),  a  liner  relation  was  simply  assumed  which resulted  in  an  extra  requirement  of  4.9%  and  9.8%  of  electricity  requirement  for  the  CO 2 removal,  respectively. Translating the additional electricity needed to capture CO2 emissions into additional amount of NG needed as a fuel, the power plant efficiency of 42% is considered. Such efficiency includes the utilizing percentage of the burned NG for the combined cycle gas turbine. For instance, at 90% capture percentage, the 14.7% increase in electricity requires  an  increase  in  the  NG  feed  stream  by  a  percentage  of  14.7%  in  addition  to  an  extra  power  due  to  the efficiency loss of the burned NG. Figure 8 presents a comparison between the quantity of NG consumed in the base case to meet the electricity demand and the 3 scenarios of CO2 capture till the year 2030.

texts.orig: Iman Ustadi et al. /   Energy Procedia   114 (  2017  )   6366 - 6376

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 8: Forecasted Natural Gas Demand (tonner/hr) Using the Capture Technology in the UAE (2014-2030)
texts.text: Figure 8: Forecasted Natural Gas Demand (tonner/hr) Using the Capture Technology in the UAE (2014-2030)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.3 Captured CO2 for EOR
texts.text: 2.2.3 Captured CO2 for EOR

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The previous presentation of CO2 capture scenarios assumes a geological sequestration of CO2 to be further stored or reused for industrial purposes. However; if the captured CO2 is used to enhance the oil recovery in the UAE, then additional effect on the demand of NG should be counted for and studied. The technique of CO2 for EOR has three advantages. First is improving the crude oil recovery and thus supporting the UAE plans to boost its oil production.  Second  is  reducing  the  ecological  foot  print  and  limiting  the  CO 2 emissions  through  the  capture technology. Third is allowing the natural gas that would be used for injection into oil fields to be freed up to meet domestic demands. As the UAE has already approved implementing the CO2-EOR technique through Al Reyadah CCUS project which will be in operation by first quarter of 2017; values from this case study will be used in our analysis. An amount of 41.5 mscft of CO2 per year will be captured from Emirates steel plants and injected in oil fields to save an amount of 35 mscft of natural gas used. Assuming full injection and using the ratio of 41.5 CO2 to 35  NG,  the  amount  of  NG  saved  in  oil  wellbores  is  calculated  in  every  capture  scenario.  Figure  9  presents  a comparison between the two situations of either using CO2 for sequestration or using it for EOR in terms of quantity of NG freed up and saved. This shows the effect of captured CO2 used for EOR in the availability of NG.
texts.text: The previous presentation of CO2 capture scenarios assumes a geological sequestration of CO2 to be further stored or reused for industrial purposes. However; if the captured CO2 is used to enhance the oil recovery in the UAE, then additional effect on the demand of NG should be counted for and studied. The technique of CO2 for EOR has three advantages. First is improving the crude oil recovery and thus supporting the UAE plans to boost its oil production.  Second  is  reducing  the  ecological  foot  print  and  limiting  the  CO 2 emissions  through  the  capture technology. Third is allowing the natural gas that would be used for injection into oil fields to be freed up to meet domestic demands. As the UAE has already approved implementing the CO2-EOR technique through Al Reyadah CCUS project which will be in operation by first quarter of 2017; values from this case study will be used in our analysis. An amount of 41.5 mscft of CO2 per year will be captured from Emirates steel plants and injected in oil fields to save an amount of 35 mscft of natural gas used. Assuming full injection and using the ratio of 41.5 CO2 to 35  NG,  the  amount  of  NG  saved  in  oil  wellbores  is  calculated  in  every  capture  scenario.  Figure  9  presents  a comparison between the two situations of either using CO2 for sequestration or using it for EOR in terms of quantity of NG freed up and saved. This shows the effect of captured CO2 used for EOR in the availability of NG.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 9: Amount of NG saved (Million metric tonnes/yr) Using CO2-EOR technique in the UAE
texts.text: Figure 9: Amount of NG saved (Million metric tonnes/yr) Using CO2-EOR technique in the UAE

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Iman Ustadi et al. /   Energy Procedia   114 (  2017  )   6366 - 6376
texts.text: Iman Ustadi et al. /   Energy Procedia   114 (  2017  )   6366 - 6376

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be observed, in the situation where CO2 is used for sequestration, an additional pressure on the aggressive demand of NG will be added due to the extra consumption of NG needed to meet the capture energy requirement. On the other, if the captured CO2 is used for EOR, additional benefit to the NG market will be applied due to the considerable  saving  quantities  of  NG  used  in  oil  wellbores.  Figure  9  illustrates,  an  amount  of  24.54,  75.44  and 126.33 million metric tonnes of NG will be freed up every year if capture percentages of 30%, 60% and 90% are applied,  respectively.  These  found  values  might  have  a  percentage  of  uncertainty  because  the  ratio  of  41.5  CO 2 captured to 35 NG saved is found for specific conditions of the oil reservoir. Although the saving quantity of NG might not shift the trend of NG imports and exports from a deficit situation into a surplus in the coming years, yet such range of deficit will get narrowed. The saving quantity of NG will reduce the heavy dependence on importing NG from Qatar, increase the opportunity of using NG for other industrial purposes and add a beneficial credit to the UAE's economy. In order to obtain accurate results for the economic benefit of NG in terms of  dollar, the cost of capture and CO2 selling price in addition to the price of NG should all be counted. This will help to investigate the commercial benefit of EOR in terms of enhancing the situation of natural gas market in the UAE.
texts.text: As can be observed, in the situation where CO2 is used for sequestration, an additional pressure on the aggressive demand of NG will be added due to the extra consumption of NG needed to meet the capture energy requirement. On the other, if the captured CO2 is used for EOR, additional benefit to the NG market will be applied due to the considerable  saving  quantities  of  NG  used  in  oil  wellbores.  Figure  9  illustrates,  an  amount  of  24.54,  75.44  and 126.33 million metric tonnes of NG will be freed up every year if capture percentages of 30%, 60% and 90% are applied,  respectively.  These  found  values  might  have  a  percentage  of  uncertainty  because  the  ratio  of  41.5  CO 2 captured to 35 NG saved is found for specific conditions of the oil reservoir. Although the saving quantity of NG might not shift the trend of NG imports and exports from a deficit situation into a surplus in the coming years, yet such range of deficit will get narrowed. The saving quantity of NG will reduce the heavy dependence on importing NG from Qatar, increase the opportunity of using NG for other industrial purposes and add a beneficial credit to the UAE's economy. In order to obtain accurate results for the economic benefit of NG in terms of  dollar, the cost of capture and CO2 selling price in addition to the price of NG should all be counted. This will help to investigate the commercial benefit of EOR in terms of enhancing the situation of natural gas market in the UAE.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Conclusion
texts.text: 3. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Looking at the energy market in the UAE, it was found that electric power production has been increasing significantly and the trend is projected to continue in the future with the dependence on hydrocarbons. As a result, the UAE will supply more gas into the gasified power plants and thus the deployment of CCS on Natural gas power plants  will  be  viable  in  reducing  the  UAE's  carbon  emissions  from  NG  power  plants.  Studying  the  effect  of deploying the CCS technology on the NG market in the UAE, it was concluded that capturing CO2 from power plant sources will result in reducing the emissions rate; yet on return increasing the power demand and the consumption of NG in the feed stream. The heaviness of such increase in demand will vary depending on the capture percentage. Moreover; if the captured CO2 is used for EOR, a considerable amount of NG will be freed up and eventually reduce the net consumption and imports of NG. In the future work, an intensive consideration with thorough study should be applied to carbon taxes, government incentives, capture cost, CO2 prices in addition to NG prices. This is because all these factors will contribute in evaluating the effect of extra electricity cost resulting from the deployment of the CCS technology, and therefore; making the right decision for the applicable capture percentage.
texts.text: Looking at the energy market in the UAE, it was found that electric power production has been increasing significantly and the trend is projected to continue in the future with the dependence on hydrocarbons. As a result, the UAE will supply more gas into the gasified power plants and thus the deployment of CCS on Natural gas power plants  will  be  viable  in  reducing  the  UAE's  carbon  emissions  from  NG  power  plants.  Studying  the  effect  of deploying the CCS technology on the NG market in the UAE, it was concluded that capturing CO2 from power plant sources will result in reducing the emissions rate; yet on return increasing the power demand and the consumption of NG in the feed stream. The heaviness of such increase in demand will vary depending on the capture percentage. Moreover; if the captured CO2 is used for EOR, a considerable amount of NG will be freed up and eventually reduce the net consumption and imports of NG. In the future work, an intensive consideration with thorough study should be applied to carbon taxes, government incentives, capture cost, CO2 prices in addition to NG prices. This is because all these factors will contribute in evaluating the effect of extra electricity cost resulting from the deployment of the CCS technology, and therefore; making the right decision for the applicable capture percentage.
